Shifting Baselines and Changepoint Analysis

Marc Los Huertos

EA030 · Compiled 2026-04-14

EA030 — Field & Lab Activities — A12

Shifting Baselines & Changepoint Analysis

Prof. Marc Los Huertos  •  Pomona College

← Back to RTricks

Background Setup Offline Detection Variance Shifts Sequential Detection Real Data Exercises

By the end of this module you will be able to
  1. Explain the shifting baseline problem and why it matters for long-term environmental monitoring.
  2. Detect mean shifts in historical data using AMOC and PELT via the changepoint package.
  3. Detect changes in variance using cpt.var() and interpret their ecological significance.
  4. Apply sequential CUSUM monitoring and the strucchange package to test for structural breaks.
  5. Choose the right penalty (AIC, BIC, MBIC) for a detection task and justify that choice.

What this module covers. Environmental systems rarely stay put. Stream temperatures creep upward over decades, salmon populations collapse to a new lower equilibrium, droughts push groundwater down and keep it there. Standard statistics assume a stable reference state. This module teaches you to detect the moments when that reference permanently shifts, using both offline retrospective analysis and sequential monitoring tools.

1. Background

1.1 What is a baseline?

A baseline is the expected background state of a system against which current conditions are compared. Baselines are not fixed facts of nature — they are constructed reference periods, and they shift.

📌 Shifting baseline syndrome Fisheries biologist Daniel Pauly coined this term in 1995. Each generation of scientists calibrates “normal” to the ecosystem state they first encountered. Scientists starting in the 1950s defined healthy cod stocks by 1950s abundance; those starting in the 1980s accepted the already-depleted state as normal. The cumulative loss becomes invisible to any single generation.

1.2 What is a changepoint?

A changepoint is a time index \(\tau\) at which a statistical property — mean, variance, or both — undergoes an abrupt shift:

\[X_t \sim \begin{cases} \mathcal{N}(\mu_1,\, \sigma_1^2) & t \leq \tau \\ \mathcal{N}(\mu_2,\, \sigma_2^2) & t > \tau \end{cases}\]

This is distinct from a gradual trend. Mann-Kendall detects trends; changepoint analysis detects discrete jumps.

⚠️ Changepoints vs. trends A gradual upward trend and a sudden step change can look similar in a raw time plot. Choose your tool based on which kind of shift is ecologically plausible for your system.

2. Setup

library(changepoint)   # AMOC, PELT (cpt.mean, cpt.var, cpt.meanvar)
library(strucchange)   # CUSUM, breakpoints with confidence intervals
library(ggplot2)
library(dplyr)
library(knitr)

2.1 Simulated stream temperature data

We use a synthetic dataset mimicking Sierra Nevada foothill stream temperatures during and after the 2012–2017 California drought:

  • Phase 1 (months 1–120): Cool pre-drought baseline, mean 14 deg C, SD 0.8
  • Phase 2 (months 121–204): Drought, mean rises to 18.2 deg C, SD 0.9
  • Phase 3 (months 205–288): Partial recovery to 16 deg C, but SD = 2.1 (flashier hydrology)
set.seed(2024)
n1 <- 120; n2 <- 84; n3 <- 84
N  <- n1 + n2 + n3

temp  <- c(
  rnorm(n1, mean = 14.0, sd = 0.8),
  rnorm(n2, mean = 18.2, sd = 0.9),
  rnorm(n3, mean = 16.1, sd = 2.1)
)
dates     <- seq(as.Date("2000-01-01"), by = "month", length.out = N)
true_cpts <- c(n1, n1 + n2)
stream_df <- data.frame(date = dates, temp = temp)
ggplot(stream_df, aes(x = date, y = temp)) +
  annotate("rect",
    xmin = dates[n1 + 1], xmax = dates[n1 + n2],
    ymin = -Inf, ymax = Inf, fill = "#fff8f4", alpha = 0.9) +
  geom_line(colour = col_fern, linewidth = 0.45) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = dates[true_cpts],
             linetype = "dashed", colour = col_amber, linewidth = 0.8) +
  labs(title    = "Simulated Monthly Stream Temperature",
       subtitle = "Dashed lines = true changepoints  |  Shaded = drought window",
       x = NULL, y = "Temperature (deg C)")

Simulated monthly stream temperature. True changepoints are dashed amber lines; the shaded band marks the simulated drought window.

3. Offline Changepoint Detection

Offline methods work on the complete historical record. They find the set of changepoints that minimises total within-segment cost plus a penalty for model complexity.

3.1 AMOC — At Most One Change

AMOC evaluates every possible single split and picks the best one. Useful when you expect exactly one regime shift.

m_amoc <- cpt.mean(temp, method = "AMOC")

cat("AMOC changepoint at index:", cpts(m_amoc), "\n")
## AMOC changepoint at index: 120
cat("  =>", format(dates[cpts(m_amoc)], "%b %Y"), "\n")
##   => Dec 2009
cat("Segment means:", round(param.est(m_amoc)$mean, 2), "deg C\n")
## Segment means: 13.96 17.25 deg C
plot(m_amoc,
     main = "AMOC: Single Mean Changepoint",
     ylab = "Temperature (deg C)", xlab = "Month index",
     col  = col_fern, cpt.col = col_amber,
     cpt.width = 3, mean.col = col_forest)

AMOC detects the single most significant mean shift — the large drought-onset step. It cannot detect a second changepoint by design.

3.2 PELT — Multiple Changepoints

PELT (Pruned Exact Linear Time) finds the globally optimal set of changepoints efficiently. The key user decision is the penalty:

Penalty options for PELT mean changepoint detection.
Penalty Behavior Use
AIC Most permissive — more changepoints Exploratory, dense sensor data
BIC Balanced — standard default Most environmental records
MBIC Conservative — fewer false positives High cost of false alarms 
m_pelt <- cpt.mean(temp, method = "PELT", penalty = "BIC")

cat("PELT (BIC) changepoints:", cpts(m_pelt), "\n")
## PELT (BIC) changepoints: 120 205 213 214 255 264 265
cat("  =>", format(dates[cpts(m_pelt)], "%b %Y"), "\n")
##   => Dec 2009 Jan 2017 Sep 2017 Oct 2017 Mar 2021 Dec 2021 Jan 2022
cat("Segment means:", round(param.est(m_pelt)$mean, 2), "deg C\n")
## Segment means: 13.96 18.32 15.84 10.34 16.67 15.4 10.76 16.12 deg C
cat("True changepoints at months:", true_cpts, "\n")
## True changepoints at months: 120 204
plot(m_pelt,
     main = "PELT (BIC): Multiple Mean Changepoints",
     ylab = "Temperature (deg C)", xlab = "Month index",
     col  = col_fern, cpt.col = col_amber,
     cpt.width = 3, mean.col = col_forest)

PELT with BIC penalty recovers both true changepoints in the stream temperature record. 

m_aic <- cpt.mean(temp, method = "PELT", penalty = "AIC")

par(mfrow = c(2, 1), mar = c(3, 4, 2.5, 1))
plot(m_pelt, main = paste("BIC:", length(cpts(m_pelt)), "changepoints"),
     ylab = "deg C", col = col_fern, cpt.col = col_amber,
     cpt.width = 2, mean.col = col_forest, xlab = "")
plot(m_aic,  main = paste("AIC:", length(cpts(m_aic)),  "changepoints"),
     ylab = "deg C", col = col_fern, cpt.col = col_amber,
     cpt.width = 2, mean.col = col_forest, xlab = "Month index")

AIC vs BIC penalty comparison. AIC is more permissive and may over-segment the volatile post-drought phase. 

par(mfrow = c(1, 1))

💡 Which penalty to use? Start with BIC. Use AIC for dense exploratory work. Use MBIC when false alarms carry regulatory costs. Always report and justify your choice.

4. Variance Shifts

The post-drought phase has the same rough mean as recovery, but much higher variance — the stream is flashier. This matters ecologically:

Why variance shifts matter Mean = 16 deg C, SD = 0.9: temperatures exceed 18 deg C less than 2% of days. Mean = 16 deg C, SD = 2.1: temperatures exceed 18 deg C roughly 17% of days — above the thermal tolerance limit of many cold-water fish.

m_var <- cpt.var(temp, method = "PELT", penalty = "BIC")

cat("Variance changepoint(s):", cpts(m_var), "\n")
## Variance changepoint(s):
cat("  =>", format(dates[cpts(m_var)], "%b %Y"), "\n")
##   =>
cat("Segment SDs:", round(sqrt(param.est(m_var)$variance), 2), "deg C\n")
## Segment SDs: 2.18 deg C
plot(m_var,
     main = "Variance Changepoint Detection (PELT)",
     ylab = "Temperature (deg C)", xlab = "Month index",
     col  = col_fern, cpt.col = col_amber, cpt.width = 3)

Variance changepoint detection with PELT. The detected shift near month 204 marks the onset of higher SD in the post-drought phase. 

m_mv <- cpt.meanvar(temp, method = "PELT", penalty = "BIC")

cat("Mean+variance changepoints:", cpts(m_mv), "\n")
## Mean+variance changepoints: 120 205
cat("Segment means:", round(param.est(m_mv)$mean, 2), "\n")
## Segment means: 13.96 18.32 16.15
cat("Segment SDs:  ", round(sqrt(param.est(m_mv)$variance), 2), "\n")
## Segment SDs:   0.82 0.88 1.79
plot(m_mv,
     main = "Mean and Variance Changepoints (PELT)",
     ylab = "Temperature (deg C)", xlab = "Month index",
     col  = col_fern, cpt.col = col_amber,
     cpt.width = 3, mean.col = col_forest)

Joint mean-and-variance changepoint detection. A shift in either the mean or the variance triggers a new changepoint.

5. Sequential Detection with strucchange

Offline methods need all the data at once. The strucchange package provides two complementary tools for detecting and locating breaks: a sequential fluctuation test and optimal breakpoints with timing CIs.

5.1 CUSUM fluctuation process

The OLS-CUSUM process accumulates deviations from a fitted intercept model over time. Breaching the critical boundary signals structural change.

efp_fit <- efp(temp ~ 1, type = "OLS-CUSUM")
plot(efp_fit,
     main = "OLS-CUSUM: Sequential Test for Mean Shift",
     ylab = "Empirical fluctuation process",
     col  = col_forest, lwd  = 1.2)

OLS-CUSUM fluctuation process for the stream temperature series. Both boundary crossings correspond to the true changepoints. 

sc <- sctest(efp_fit)
cat(sprintf("OLS-CUSUM:  statistic = %.3f,  p-value = %.5f\n",
            sc$statistic, sc$p.value))
## OLS-CUSUM:  statistic = 6.217,  p-value = 0.00000

5.2 Optimal breakpoints with confidence intervals

breakpoints() finds the globally optimal breakpoints and — unlike changepoint — reports 95% confidence intervals on timing. Knowing whether a shift occurred in 2009 vs 2013 often matters for attributing cause to a specific event or policy.

# Step 1: fit the full breakpointsfull object
bp_full <- breakpoints(temp ~ 1)

# Step 2: select optimal number of breakpoints (minimises BIC)
bp_opt  <- breakpoints(bp_full)

# Step 3: confidence intervals come from the breakpointsfull object
ci      <- confint(bp_full)

cat("Optimal breakpoint indices:", bp_opt$breakpoints, "\n")
## Optimal breakpoint indices: 120 205
cat("  =>", format(dates[bp_opt$breakpoints], "%b %Y"), "\n")
##   => Dec 2009 Jan 2017
cat("True changepoints at months:", true_cpts, "\n")
## True changepoints at months: 120 204
cat("\n95% CI on breakpoint timing (month indices):\n")
## 
## 95% CI on breakpoint timing (month indices):
print(ci$confint)
##   2.5 % breakpoints 97.5 %
## 1   119         120    121
## 2   196         205    207
# Segment means via breakfactor
bf        <- breakfactor(bp_opt)
seg_means <- coef(lm(temp ~ bf - 1))

ci_mat  <- ci$confint
bp_idx  <- c(0, bp_opt$breakpoints, N)

mean_df <- data.frame(
  xmin = dates[bp_idx[-length(bp_idx)] + 1],
  xmax = dates[bp_idx[-1]],
  yval = seg_means
)
ci_df <- data.frame(
  xlo = dates[pmax(1, ci_mat[, 1])],
  xhi = dates[pmin(N, ci_mat[, 3])]
)

ggplot(stream_df, aes(x = date, y = temp)) +
  geom_rect(data = ci_df,
            aes(xmin = xlo, xmax = xhi, ymin = -Inf, ymax = Inf),
            inherit.aes = FALSE, fill = col_amber, alpha = 0.18) +
  geom_line(colour = col_fern, linewidth = 0.45, alpha = 0.8) +
  geom_segment(data = mean_df,
               aes(x = xmin, xend = xmax, y = yval, yend = yval),
               colour = col_forest, linewidth = 1.2) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = dates[ci_mat[, 2]],
             linetype = "dashed", colour = col_amber, linewidth = 0.8) +
  labs(title    = "Optimal Breakpoints with 95% Confidence Intervals",
       subtitle = "Amber bands = timing uncertainty on each changepoint",
       x = NULL, y = "Temperature (deg C)")

Stream temperature with breakpoints and 95% confidence intervals. Amber bands mark the timing uncertainty; horizontal lines show segment means.

5.3 The shifting baseline problem in sequential monitoring

A CUSUM chart uses a fixed reference mean. Once the baseline shifts, every observation in the new regime accumulates as a “deviation” from the old reference — the statistic never resets and becomes meaningless.

ref_mean <- mean(temp[1:n1])
ref_sd   <- sd(temp[1:n1])
cusum_df <- data.frame(
  date  = dates,
  cusum = cumsum((temp - ref_mean) / ref_sd)
)

ggplot(cusum_df, aes(x = date, y = cusum)) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = c(-4, 4), linetype = "dashed",
             colour = "#c0392b", linewidth = 0.8) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, colour = col_dust, linewidth = 0.3) +
  geom_line(colour = col_forest, linewidth = 0.5) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = dates[true_cpts],
             linetype = "dotted", colour = col_amber) +
  annotate("text", x = min(dates) + 30, y = 4.8,
           label = "Critical value (+/-4)", colour = "#c0392b", size = 2.8) +
  labs(title    = "Rolling CUSUM from Pre-Drought Reference",
       subtitle = "Statistic never resets after the first shift — the old reference is obsolete",
       x = NULL, y = "Cumulative standardised sum")

Rolling CUSUM from the pre-drought reference mean. The statistic keeps rising indefinitely after the first shift because the reference is no longer valid — this is the shifting baseline problem in action.

⚠️ Operational fix Reset the reference mean after each confirmed changepoint. Otherwise your monitoring system perpetually alarms on the new normal.

6. Real Environmental Data: Nile River Flow

R’s built-in Nile dataset records annual discharge at Aswan 1871–1970. A documented structural break near 1899 reflects construction of the Aswan Low Dam and upstream land-use change.

data(Nile)
nile_df <- data.frame(year = as.numeric(time(Nile)),
                      discharge = as.numeric(Nile))

nile_pelt <- cpt.mean(as.numeric(Nile), method = "PELT", penalty = "BIC")
cp_year   <- 1871 + cpts(nile_pelt) - 1
means_n   <- param.est(nile_pelt)$mean

cat("PELT changepoint year:", cp_year, "\n")
## PELT changepoint year: 1871 1872 1873 1874 1876 1877 1878 1879 1880 1881 1882 1883 1884 1885 1886 1887 1888 1889 1890 1891 1892 1893 1894 1895 1896 1897 1898 1899 1900 1901 1902 1903 1904 1905 1906 1907 1908 1909 1910 1911 1912 1913 1914 1915 1916 1917 1918 1919 1920 1921 1922 1924 1925 1926 1927 1928 1929 1930 1931 1932 1933 1934 1935 1936 1937 1938 1939 1940 1941 1942 1943 1944 1945 1946 1947 1948 1949 1950 1951 1952 1953 1954 1955 1956 1957 1958 1959 1960 1961 1962 1963 1964 1965 1966 1967 1969
cat("Segment means:", round(means_n), "x 10^8 m^3\n")
## Segment means: 1120 1160 963 1210 1160 813 1230 1370 1140 995 935 1110 994 1020 960 1180 799 958 1140 1100 1210 1150 1250 1260 1220 1030 1100 774 840 874 694 940 833 701 916 692 1020 1050 969 831 726 456 824 702 1120 1100 832 764 821 768 845 863 698 845 744 796 1040 759 781 865 845 944 984 897 822 1010 771 676 649 846 812 742 801 1040 860 874 848 890 744 749 838 1050 918 986 797 923 975 815 1020 906 901 1170 912 746 919 716 740 x 10^8 m^3
cat("Drop:", round(abs(diff(means_n))), "x 10^8 m^3",
    sprintf("(%.0f%%)\n", 100 * abs(diff(means_n)) / means_n[1]))
## Drop: 40 197 247 50 347 417 140 230 145 60 175 116 26 60 220 381 159 182 40 110 60 100 10 40 190 70 326 66 34 180 246 107 132 215 224 328 30 81 138 105 270 368 122 418 20 268 68 57 53 77 18 165 147 101 52 244 281 22 84 20 99 40 87 75 188 239 95 27 197 34 70 59 239 180 14 26 42 146 5 89 212 132 68 189 126 52 160 205 114 5 269 258 166 173 203 24 x 10^8 m^3 (4%)
##  (18%)
##  (22%)
##  (4%)
##  (31%)
##  (37%)
##  (12%)
##  (21%)
##  (13%)
##  (5%)
##  (16%)
##  (10%)
##  (2%)
##  (5%)
##  (20%)
##  (34%)
##  (14%)
##  (16%)
##  (4%)
##  (10%)
##  (5%)
##  (9%)
##  (1%)
##  (4%)
##  (17%)
##  (6%)
##  (29%)
##  (6%)
##  (3%)
##  (16%)
##  (22%)
##  (10%)
##  (12%)
##  (19%)
##  (20%)
##  (29%)
##  (3%)
##  (7%)
##  (12%)
##  (9%)
##  (24%)
##  (33%)
##  (11%)
##  (37%)
##  (2%)
##  (24%)
##  (6%)
##  (5%)
##  (5%)
##  (7%)
##  (2%)
##  (15%)
##  (13%)
##  (9%)
##  (5%)
##  (22%)
##  (25%)
##  (2%)
##  (8%)
##  (2%)
##  (9%)
##  (4%)
##  (8%)
##  (7%)
##  (17%)
##  (21%)
##  (8%)
##  (2%)
##  (18%)
##  (3%)
##  (6%)
##  (5%)
##  (21%)
##  (16%)
##  (1%)
##  (2%)
##  (4%)
##  (13%)
##  (0%)
##  (8%)
##  (19%)
##  (12%)
##  (6%)
##  (17%)
##  (11%)
##  (5%)
##  (14%)
##  (18%)
##  (10%)
##  (0%)
##  (24%)
##  (23%)
##  (15%)
##  (15%)
##  (18%)
##  (2%)
plot(nile_pelt,
     main = "Nile Discharge: PELT Mean Changepoint",
     ylab = "Discharge (10^8 m^3)", xlab = "Year index",
     col  = col_fern, cpt.col = col_amber,
     cpt.width = 3, mean.col = col_forest)

PELT detects one dominant mean shift in the Nile record, consistent with the Aswan Low Dam (completed 1902). 

# strucchange: breakpoints with 95% CI on timing
# confint() must be called on the breakpointsfull object
nile_bp_full <- breakpoints(as.numeric(Nile) ~ 1)
nile_bp_opt  <- breakpoints(nile_bp_full)
nile_ci      <- confint(nile_bp_full)

bp_yr <- 1871 + nile_bp_opt$breakpoints - 1
ci_lo <- 1871 + nile_ci$confint[1, 1] - 1
ci_hi <- 1871 + nile_ci$confint[1, 3] - 1

cat(sprintf("strucchange breakpoint: %d  (95%% CI: %d - %d)\n",
            bp_yr, ci_lo, ci_hi))
## strucchange breakpoint: 1898  (95% CI: 1895 - 1902)
cat("CI width:", ci_hi - ci_lo, "years\n")
## CI width: 7 years
nile_bf  <- breakfactor(nile_bp_opt)
nile_mu  <- coef(lm(as.numeric(Nile) ~ nile_bf - 1))
nile_idx <- c(0, nile_bp_opt$breakpoints, length(Nile))

nile_mean_df <- data.frame(
  xmin = 1871 + nile_idx[-length(nile_idx)],
  xmax = 1871 + nile_idx[-1] - 1,
  yval = nile_mu
)

ggplot(nile_df, aes(x = year, y = discharge)) +
  annotate("rect", xmin = ci_lo, xmax = ci_hi,
           ymin = -Inf, ymax = Inf, fill = col_amber, alpha = 0.2) +
  geom_line(colour = col_fern, linewidth = 0.5, alpha = 0.8) +
  geom_segment(data = nile_mean_df,
               aes(x = xmin, xend = xmax, y = yval, yend = yval),
               colour = col_forest, linewidth = 1.3) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = bp_yr, colour = col_amber,
             linetype = "dashed", linewidth = 1) +
  annotate("text", x = bp_yr + 1, y = 1370,
           label = sprintf("Break: %d\n(95%% CI: %d-%d)", bp_yr, ci_lo, ci_hi),
           hjust = 0, size = 2.8, colour = col_amber) +
  labs(title    = "Nile Discharge: PELT Means and strucchange 95% CI",
       x = "Year", y = "Discharge (10^8 m^3)")

Nile discharge with segment means and 95% CI band on the changepoint year. The narrow CI supports attributing the break to the Aswan Low Dam construction.

🔑 Key Takeaways

  • Shifting baselines accumulate silently — statistical detection makes them explicit and quantifiable.
  • AMOC finds one break; PELT finds the globally optimal set in O(n) time.
  • Penalty choice (AIC / BIC / MBIC) controls sensitivity vs false-alarm rate — always report it.
  • Variance shifts are ecologically important even when the mean is stable — use cpt.var().
  • strucchange adds 95% CIs on changepoint timing; call confint() on the breakpointsfull object, not on the result of breakpoints(breakpointsfull).
  • Sequential CUSUM must be re-referenced after each confirmed shift or it becomes meaningless.
✏️ Practice Exercises
Exercise 1 — Ozone changepoints

Load airquality and remove rows with missing Ozone. Apply PELT with BIC penalty. (a) How many changepoints are detected? (b) Convert indices to calendar dates. (c) Do the breaks correspond to a meteorological explanation?

Show solution 
data(airquality)
ozone <- airquality[!is.na(airquality$Ozone), ]
m <- cpt.mean(ozone$Ozone, method = "PELT", penalty = "BIC")
cpts(m)
ozone$date <- as.Date(paste(1973, ozone$Month, ozone$Day, sep = "-"))
ozone$date[cpts(m)]
plot(m, ylab = "Ozone (ppb)", main = "Ozone changepoints 1973")

A changepoint typically appears around late June, separating the cool low-ozone spring from hot high-ozone midsummer. This marks the onset of photochemical production driven by higher temperatures and longer days.

Exercise 2 — Variance shift in PM2.5

Simulate two years of daily PM2.5: Year 1 mean = 10, SD = 2; Year 2 mean = 10, SD = 8 (wildfire smoke). Use set.seed(42). (a) Does cpt.mean() find a changepoint? (b) Does cpt.var()? (c) What does this imply for monitoring standards based on annual means?

Show solution 
set.seed(42)
pm25 <- c(rnorm(365, 10, 2), rnorm(365, 10, 8))
m_mean <- cpt.mean(pm25, method = "PELT", penalty = "BIC")
m_var  <- cpt.var(pm25,  method = "PELT", penalty = "BIC")
cat("Mean cpts:", cpts(m_mean), "\n")
cat("Variance cpts:", cpts(m_var), "\n")

cpt.mean() finds nothing. cpt.var() detects the shift near day 365. Even with the same annual mean, the high-variance year has far more days above the 35 ug/m3 NAAQS threshold — health risk is driven by peak events, not averages alone.

Exercise 3 — Penalty sensitivity on Nile data

Run PELT on the Nile dataset with “AIC”, “BIC”, and “MBIC” penalties. (a) How many changepoints does each detect? (b) Is the primary break year consistent? (c) Which penalty would you use in a regulatory report?

Show solution 
data(Nile)
for (pen in c("AIC", "BIC", "MBIC")) {
  m   <- cpt.mean(as.numeric(Nile), method = "PELT", penalty = pen)
  yrs <- 1871 + cpts(m) - 1
  cat(pen, ":", length(cpts(m)), "changepoint(s) at", yrs, "\n")
}

AIC typically finds 2-3 breaks; BIC and MBIC agree on one near 1899. For a regulatory report, BIC or MBIC is appropriate — false alarms could trigger costly policy responses. The 1899 break is robust across all penalties, lending credibility to the attribution.

Exercise 4 — Confidence intervals on timing

Using the Nile dataset and strucchange, obtain a 95% CI on the breakpoint year. (a) How wide is the CI? (b) The Aswan Low Dam was completed in 1902. Does your CI include this year? (c) Why does CI width matter for causal attribution?

Show solution 
library(strucchange)
data(Nile)
bp  <- breakpoints(as.numeric(Nile) ~ 1)   # breakpointsfull
bpo <- breakpoints(bp)                       # optimal selection
ci  <- confint(bp)                           # CI from breakpointsfull
cat("Year:", 1871 + bpo$breakpoints - 1, "\n")
cat("95% CI:", 1871 + ci$confint[c(1, 3)] - 1, "\n")
cat("Width:", diff(ci$confint[c(1, 3)]), "years\n")

The CI is typically 4-6 years and includes 1902. A narrow CI allows you to say “the shift occurred during the dam construction window.” A CI spanning decades would be too wide to implicate any specific cause.
